planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist perlite/peat mix or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade after last frost; crossandra blooms nearly year-round in warm, humid, frost-free conditions.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect light to partial shade; crossandra is an Indian native grown for its salmon-orange to coral-red flowers borne in four-sided spikes nearly year-round in warm, frost-free conditions, making it one of the most continuously blooming ornamentals for shaded areas in tropical and subtropical landscapes. Plants prefer consistent warmth and humidity — they struggle when temperatures drop below 55°F and require protection from direct afternoon sun in hot climates. In the Gulf South grow as a container plant, bringing indoors in winter. Fertilize monthly during active growth. The flowers make excellent leis in Hawaii.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer in moist perlite or plant container specimens on a trellis in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect light to partial shade; stephanotis requires consistent warmth and resents root disturbance.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens on a trellis in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect light to partial shade; stephanotis is a twining vine from Madagascar prized for its waxy, pure-white, intensely fragrant tubular flowers in clusters from spring through summer — the flowers have one of the most exquisite perfumes in the plant kingdom and are traditionally used in bridal bouquets and leis. Keep the root zone consistently moist but never waterlogged. The vine requires a period of cool, dry rest in winter (55–60°F) to set flower buds for the following season. Plants resent being moved once established; keep in the same pot or location for best performance. Grow outdoors year-round only in frost-free zones.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist potting mix or plant container specimens in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to bright indirect light after last frost; bleeding heart vine blooms most heavily after a period of cool, dry rest.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to bright indirect light; bleeding heart vine is a West African twining plant grown for its striking bicolor flowers — a pure white, inflated, heart-shaped calyx that persists for weeks and a scarlet-red tubular corolla that emerges from the center, creating a vivid crimson-and-white effect in large clusters from spring through summer. After bloom, allow the plant to rest in a cool (60°F), drier period in fall to promote flowering the following season. Remove the rest period gradually by increasing water and temperature in late winter. Excellent trained on a trellis, arbor, or as a container specimen. Hummingbirds visit the flowers in tropical settings.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem tip cuttings in moist potting mix or plant container specimens in well-drained, organically rich soil in partial shade to full sun after last frost; schefflera grows rapidly in warm climates — up to 2–3 feet per year — and becomes a large specimen tree in frost-free zones.

directions: Root stem tip cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, organically rich soil in partial shade to full sun; umbrella plant grows rapidly to become a large, multi-stemmed tree in zones 10–12 with bold, palmate, compound leaves of 7–16 leaflets arranged like the ribs of an umbrella, creating a striking tropical silhouette. Mature trees produce dramatic, upright clusters of small red flowers that attract birds. Outdoors it can reach 40 feet tall; as a container plant or indoor specimen in filtered light it stays manageable at 4–8 feet. Plant has become invasive in Florida and Hawaii — consider the smaller, non-invasive Schefflera arboricola (dwarf schefflera) as an alternative in those states. Container plants tolerate low light exceptionally well.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun after last frost; orchid trees grow rapidly — up to 3–5 feet per year — and begin flowering within 3–5 years from seed.

directions: Start seeds in moist potting mix after soaking 24 hours, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; orchid tree is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the subtropical world, producing large, pink-to-magenta 5-petaled flowers that truly resemble orchid blossoms from late winter to spring before or as the distinctive two-lobed (camel's foot-shaped) leaves emerge. Trees grow rapidly and begin flowering in 3–5 years, reaching 20–30 feet at maturity. They are relatively cold-hardy compared to other tropical trees, tolerating brief freezes to 22°F once established. Young trees require frost protection in zone 9 until the trunk reaches 4–5 inches in diameter. Prune after bloom to shape and remove any frost-damaged wood.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist potting mix or plant container specimens near a strong trellis, fence, or arbor in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pink trumpet vine blooms most heavily in fall and early winter in Gulf South climates.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens on a sturdy trellis, pergola, or fence in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pink trumpet vine is a South African twining vine that produces large clusters of soft pink, trumpet-shaped flowers with red-striped throats from fall through early winter, filling a bloom gap when most vines have stopped flowering. The pinnate, deep-green glossy leaves are attractive throughout the growing season. The vine grows moderately (10–20 feet), is much less aggressive than Campsis radicans, and is suitable for structures that cannot support heavy, vigorous vines. Drought-tolerant once established. In zone 9 cut back frost-damaged stems in late winter; plants re-sprout reliably from the base.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm, moist potting mix or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; golden dewdrop blooms heavily in summer and continues into fall in Gulf South landscapes.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; golden dewdrop is a tropical shrub or small tree from the Caribbean and Mexico that simultaneously displays two ornamental seasons — racemes of lavender-blue flowers with white margins and strings of golden-yellow, cherry-sized berries, giving the plant both its common names. Butterflies and hummingbirds visit the flowers eagerly; songbirds eat the berries. Plants bloom and fruit almost continuously in warm climates, reaching 15 feet unpruned. Prune heavily after bloom to control size and promote dense growth. Note: the berries are toxic to humans and pets. In zone 8 cut back frost-damaged stems in late winter — plants re-sprout vigorously from the roots.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix in warmth and humidity, or plant container specimens in moist, fertile, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade to bright indirect light after last frost; sanchezia colors most intensely in bright indirect light.

directions: Root stem cuttings easily in moist potting mix in a warm, humid environment, or plant container specimens in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect light to partial shade; sanchezia is a Peruvian rainforest shrub grown for its dramatically large, glossy leaves with bold yellow or white veins that glow against the deep green leaf surface — a stunning foliage plant that makes a strong tropical statement in shaded landscapes. Spikes of red-and-yellow tubular flowers appear intermittently. Plants prefer consistently moist, warm conditions and do not tolerate cold, dry winds or temperatures below 50°F. In zone 9 grow in large containers and bring indoors before frost to a warm, bright location. Fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer during active growth.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
